perturbation.original_text: The Company shall reimburse the Consultant for all reasonable and necessary documented out of pocket expenses incurred or paid by the Consultant in connection with, or related to, the performance of Consultant's services under this Agreement. The Consultant shall submit to the Company itemized monthly statements, in a form satisfactory to the Company, of such expenses incurred in the previous month. The Company shall pay to the Consultant amounts shown on each such statement within thirty (30) days after receipt thereof.
perturbation.changed_text: The Company may reimburse the Consultant for documented out of pocket expenses incurred or paid by the Consultant in connection with, or related to, the performance of Consultant's services under this Agreement, subject to internal approval policies. The Consultant shall submit to the Company statements of such expenses incurred, with the level of detail deemed appropriate by the Consultant. The Company shall endeavor to pay the Consultant amounts shown on each such statement within a reasonable timeframe.
perturbation.explanation: The changes introduce ambiguity by replacing 'shall reimburse' with 'may reimburse,' making reimbursement discretionary. 'Subject to internal approval policies' adds an undefined condition. The phrase 'level of detail deemed appropriate by the Consultant' removes the requirement for itemized statements, and 'endeavor to pay within a reasonable timeframe' replaces the clear 30-day payment period with a vague commitment. This creates uncertainty regarding expense reimbursement.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Massachusetts Wage Act regarding timely payment of expenses
perturbation.law_explanation: The Massachusetts Wage Act (M.G.L. c. 149 § 148) mandates the prompt payment of wages, which can include reimbursements for expenses incurred by employees or consultants in performing their duties. By making reimbursement discretionary and introducing vague terms like 'reasonable timeframe,' the modified text contradicts the spirit of the law, which requires clear and timely payment.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: General Law - Part I, Title XXI, Chapter 149, Section 148 Skip to Content General Laws Part I ADMINISTRATION OF THE GOVERNMENT Title XXI LABOR AND INDUSTRIES Chapter 149 LABOR AND INDUSTRIES Section 148 Payment of wages; commissions; exemption by contract; persons deemed employers; provision for cashing check or draft; violation of statute Section 148 Search Search the Legislature Search General Laws Go Directly to a General Law Chapter Section Go to General Law Skip to Content Print Page Prev ious Next Section 148: Payment of wages; commissions; exemption by contract; persons deemed employers; provision for cashing check or draft; violation of statute Section 148. Every person having employees in his service shall pay weekly or bi-weekly each such employee the wages earned by him to within six days of the termination of the pay period during which the wages were earned if employed for five or six days in a calendar week, or to within seven days of the termination of the pay period during which the wages were earned if such employee is employed seven days in a calendar week, or in the case of an employee who has worked for a period of less than five days, hereinafter called a casual employee, shall, within seven days after the termination of such period, pay the wages earned by such casual employee during such period, but any employee leaving his employment shall be paid in full on the following regular pay day, and, in the absence of a regular pay day, on the following Saturday; and any employee discharged from such employment shall be paid in full on the day of his discharge, or in Boston as soon as the laws requiring pay rolls, bills and accounts to be certified shall have been complied with; and the commonwealth, its departments, officers, boards and commissions shall so pay every mechanic, workman and laborer employed by it or them, and every person employed in any other capacity by it or them in any penal or charitable institution, and every county and city shall so pay every employee engaged in its business the wag
perturbation.justification: The modified contract introduces ambiguity and discretion where the original text was definitive, potentially contradicting Massachusetts wage law. The original text uses 'shall reimburse,' creating a clear obligation for the company to reimburse the consultant. The changed text uses 'may reimburse,' making reimbursement discretionary and 'endeavor to pay' replacing a 30-day deadline with a vague commitment. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 refers to 'payment of wages,' the Law Explanation asserts that this encompasses reimbursements. By introducing discretionary language and indefinite timeframes, the modified text arguably undermines the prompt payment expectation which the law intends to impose.

perturbation.original_text: The Consultant agrees that all files, documents, letters, memoranda, reports, records, data, sketches, drawings, models, laboratory notebooks, program listings, computer equipment or devices, computer programs or other written, photographic, or other tangible material containing Proprietary Information, whether created by the Consultant or others, which shall come into Consultant's custody or possession, shall be and are the exclusive property of the Company to be used by the Consultant only in the performance of Consultant's duties for the Company and shall not be copied or removed from the Company premises except in the pursuit of the business of the Company. All such materials or copies thereof and all tangible property of the Company in the custody or possession of the Consultant shall be delivered to the Company, upon the earlier of (i) a request by the Company or (ii) the termination of this Agreement. After such delivery, the Consultant shall not retain any such materials or copies thereof or any such tangible property.
perturbation.changed_text: The Consultant agrees that documents and material containing Proprietary Information, which shall come into Consultant's custody or possession, are the property of the Company to be used by the Consultant in the performance of Consultant's duties for the Company. Such materials shall be delivered to the Company upon termination of this Agreement to the extent reasonably practicable.
perturbation.explanation: This change significantly weakens the obligation to return proprietary information. By removing the comprehensive list of what constitutes 'documents and material' and adding the caveat 'to the extent reasonably practicable,' the consultant has significant leeway in deciding what to return and whether returning it is feasible. This makes it difficult for the company to enforce the return of its proprietary information.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Massachusetts Trade Secrets Act (M.G.L. c. 93 § 42) provides legal remedies for the misappropriation of trade secrets. By making the return of proprietary information discretionary ('to the extent reasonably practicable'), the modified text weakens the company's ability to protect its trade secrets and increases the risk of misappropriation. This ambiguity reduces the enforceability of the agreement under the Trade Secrets Act.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: General Law - Part I, Title XV, Chapter 93, Section 42 Skip to Content General Laws Part I ADMINISTRATION OF THE GOVERNMENT Title XV REGULATION OF TRADE Chapter 93 REGULATION OF TRADE AND CERTAIN ENTERPRISES Section 42 Trade secrets; definitions applicable to Secs. 42 to 42G Section 42 Search Search the Legislature Search General Laws Go Directly to a General Law Chapter Section Go to General Law Skip to Content Print Page Prev ious Next Section 42: Trade secrets; definitions applicable to Secs. 42 to 42G [Text of section applicable as provided by 2018, 228, Sec. 70.] Section 42. As used in this section and in sections 42A to 42G, inclusive, the following words, shall unless the context clearly requires otherwise, have the following meanings: (1) ''Improper means'', includes, without limitation, theft, bribery, misrepresentation, unreasonable intrusion into private physical or electronic space, or breach or inducement of a breach of a confidential relationship or other duty to limit acquisition, disclosure or use of information; reverse engineering from properly accessed materials or information is not improper means. (2) ''Misappropriation'', (i) an act of acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or who has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (ii) an act of disclosure or of use of a trade secret of another without that person's express or implied consent by a person who (A) used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret or (B) at the time of the actor's disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that the actor's knowledge of the trade secret was (I) derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (II) acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to limit its acquisition, disclosure, or use; or (III) derived from or through a person who owed a duty to the person seeking relief to limit its acquisition, disclosure, or use; or (C) before a material change of the actor's position, knew or had reason to know tha
perturbation.justification: The modified contract introduces ambiguity regarding the return of proprietary information, weakening the company's protection of trade secrets. The original text mandates the return of all specified materials, whereas the changed text adds 'to the extent reasonably practicable,' making the obligation discretionary. This directly undermines the Massachusetts Trade Secrets Act (M.G.L. c. 93 § 42), which aims to protect against misappropriation. As indicated in Scraped Law Snippet 1, misappropriation includes the acquisition of trade secrets by improper means, or the disclosure/use of a trade secret without consent. By creating leeway in the return of proprietary information, the amended contract increases the risk of misappropriation, contradicting the intent and enforceability of trade secret protections.
